file_name: Malhotra and Kaur - 2024 - Exploring Explainable Melanoma Classification Leveraging Pre-trained Deep Learning Model on MED-NOD.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (InceptionV3, Xception, VGG19, EfficientNetB1, and DenseNet201) via transfer learning to classify melanoma and naevus images from the MED-NODE dataset. It also employed Explainable AI (LIME) to visualize lesion sections.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from classifying melanoma and naevus images using five pre-trained deep learning models (InceptionV3, Xception, VGG19, EfficientNetB1, and DenseNet201) on the MED-NODE dataset. It includes a methodology section detailing data preprocessing and model training, a results section with specific performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC), and a statistical analysis (z-scores, p-values) comparing model performances. Although published in a conference proceedings (INDIACom), it reports new empirical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Exploring Explainable Melanoma Classification: Leveraging Pre-trained Deep Learning Model on MED-NODE Dataset
journal: 2024 11th International Conference on Computing for Sustainable Global Development (INDIACom)
brief_summary: This study evaluated five pre-trained deep learning models (InceptionV3, Xception, VGG19, EfficientNetB1, and DenseNet201) for classifying melanoma and naevus images from the MED-NODE dataset. The models were assessed using transfer learning with performance metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC. DenseNet201 achieved the highest accuracy of 96.47%, followed by Xception at 95.88%, while VGG19 performed the poorest at 54.7%. Statistical analysis using z-scores and two-tailed p-values confirmed significant performance differences among the top three models. Additionally, Explainable AI (LIME) was employed to visualize lesion sections, enhancing model interpretability for dermatological diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classification classes in the MED-NODE dataset, consisting of 70 images.
One of the two binary classification classes in the MED-NODE dataset, consisting of 100 images.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into 'melanoma' and 'naevus' classes
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'dermatological image classification' and 'classification of melanoma images'. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. naevus) using the MED-NODE dataset. Performance metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC for these binary classes.

datasets_used.name: MED-NODE
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of macroscopic skin lesion images obtained from the digital image library of the Department of Dermatology at the University Medical Center Groningen (UMCG). The images have varied dimensions ranging from 201 x 257 pixels to 3177 x 1333 pixels. It was used for the advancement and assessment of the MED-NODE computer-assisted system for detecting skin cancer.

use.training: Used for training five pre-trained deep learning models (InceptionV3, Xception, VGG19, EfficientNetB1, and DenseNet201) via transfer learning.
use.validation: Used for evaluating model performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC) and statistical analysis (z-scores, p-values).
use.testing: Used for final performance assessment and comparison with existing models (e.g., PECK Ensemble).
use.other: Used for Explainable AI (LIME) visualization of lesion sections.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the top three models (EfficientNetB1, Xception, and DenseNet201) by indicating their standard deviation from the mean performance metrics.
To confirm the statistical significance of the performance differences among the top three models and assess the reliability of the results.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in performance among the top three models (EfficientNetB1, Xception, and DenseNet201).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in performance among the top three models (EfficientNetB1, Xception, and DenseNet201).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The two-tailed p-values for the z-scores of the top three models were all less than 0.001, indicating a very low probability of random chance and confirming that the changes in model performance indicators are statistically significant.

MED-NODE.notes: DenseNet201 achieved the highest accuracy (96.47%). Xception and EfficientNetB1 also showed high performance with perfect precision, recall, and F1-scores. VGG19 performed the poorest. The proposed DenseNet201 model showed a substantial 5% performance improvement over the existing PECK Ensemble model. Statistical analysis using z-scores and two-tailed p-values confirmed significant differences among the top three models (EfficientNetB1, Xception, and DenseNet201), with p-values < 0.001. Z-scores were: EfficientNetB1 (-44.46), Xception (-62.26), and DenseNet201 (-52.45). The mean AUC on the MED-NODE dataset was 0.9702.

architecture_used.main_model: Five pre-trained deep learning models: InceptionV3, Xception, VGG19, EfficientNetB1, and DenseNet201. DenseNet201 is highlighted as the primary proposed model.
architecture_used.pretraining: All models were pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset and used via transfer learning (fine-tuning).

architecture_details.Xception: Pre-trained CNN architecture utilizing depthwise separable convolutions instead of conventional convolutional layers.
architecture_details.EfficientNetB1: Pre-trained CNN architecture from the EfficientNet family, utilizing compound scaling to equalize depth, width, and resolution.
architecture_details.DenseNet201: Pre-trained DenseNet variant with 201 layers, characterized by dense connectivity patterns where each layer receives input from all antecedent layers.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a standardized input size of 112 x 112 pixels
conclusions: DenseNet201 achieved the highest classification accuracy (96.47%) among the tested pre-trained deep learning models (InceptionV3, Xception, VGG19, EfficientNetB1, and DenseNet201) for distinguishing between melanoma and naevus images on the MED-NODE dataset.

Xception demonstrated exceptional performance with 95.88% accuracy and perfect precision, recall, and F1-scores for both classes, while EfficientNetB1 also performed robustly with 91.76% accuracy and perfect metrics.
VGG19 exhibited the poorest performance with an accuracy of 54.7%, and InceptionV3 showed a balanced trade-off with 64.7% accuracy.
The proposed DenseNet201 model demonstrated a substantial 5% performance improvement over the existing PECK Ensemble model (91.12% accuracy).

Statistical analysis using z-scores and two-tailed p-values (p < 0.001) confirmed that the performance differences among the top three models (EfficientNetB1, Xception, and DenseNet201) are statistically significant.
The mean Area Under the Curve (AUC) for the top models on the MED-NODE dataset was 0.9702, indicating robust discriminatory abilities.

The integration of Explainable AI (LIME) successfully visualized lesion sections, enhancing the interpretability of the deep learning models for dermatological diagnosis and helping practitioners identify relevant image features.
important_remarks: The study uses a very small dataset (170 images total: 70 melanoma, 100 naevus), which raises concerns about overfitting and generalizability despite the high reported accuracy.
The paper does not report a train/validation/test split; it implies using the entire MED-NODE dataset for evaluation without specifying how generalization was assessed or if data leakage occurred.

Models like Xception, EfficientNetB1, and DenseNet201 report perfect precision, recall, and F1-scores (1.0) on the test set, which is unusually high for such a small and heterogeneous clinical dataset and may indicate data leakage or an overly simple classification boundary.
The dataset images have highly variable dimensions (201x257 to 3177x1333 pixels) but were resized to 112x112 pixels, potentially losing critical diagnostic details.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, which is a significant limitation given the small sample size.
The comparison with the PECK Ensemble model is indirect, as the PECK results are cited from a separate reference [15] rather than re-evaluated on the same split or conditions in this study.
The study was conducted on Google Colab Premium, and hardware specifications are not provided, which may affect reproducibility.

The paper mentions evaluating models on a 'Faces94' validation set in the context of ROC curves (Fig. 3), but the primary results and statistical analysis are only reported for the MED-NODE dataset, creating ambiguity about external validation.

The statistical analysis uses z-scores and p-values to compare model performances, but the methodology for calculating these z-scores across such a small sample size is not fully detailed or standard for this context.

dataset_usage_details.source_pdf_path: /home/agarciag/Desktop/skin_cancer_review/llm_agent/pdfs/compliant/processed_Malhotra and Kaur - 2024 - Exploring Explainable Melanoma Classification Leveraging Pre-trained Deep Learning Model on MED-NOD.pdf
dataset_usage_details.summary: The study evaluated five pre-trained deep learning models (InceptionV3, Xception, VGG19, EfficientNetB1, and DenseNet201) for binary classification of melanoma and naevus using the MED-NODE dataset. The models were fine-tuned via transfer learning. The paper does not explicitly define a train/validation/test split, implying the entire dataset was used for evaluation or training/testing without clear separation. A mention of 'Faces94' as a validation set appears in the context of ROC curve generation but is not used for model selection or reported in the primary performance metrics.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper does not report training on one named dataset and testing on a different unseen named dataset. While 'Faces94' is mentioned in the context of ROC curves (Fig. 3), it is not used as an external test set for the primary performance evaluation reported in Table I. The primary evaluation is confined to the MED-NODE dataset.

training.notes: Used for fine-tuning the pre-trained models. The paper does not specify a separate training split, implying the dataset was used for the final evaluation or combined training/testing.

validation.notes: No explicit validation set for model selection is described. Hyperparameters (learning rate 0.001, batch size 8, 25 epochs) were fixed.

testing.notes: Used for final performance assessment. The paper reports metrics on this dataset without specifying if it was held out from training.

external_testing.notes: Mentioned in the context of generating ROC curves (Fig. 3) alongside MED-NODE. However, no performance metrics (accuracy, AUC, etc.) are reported for Faces94 in the results tables, and it is not used as a primary external test set for the main findings.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: The pre-trained models (InceptionV3, Xception, VGG19, EfficientNetB1, DenseNet201) were initialized with weights trained on the ImageNet dataset before fine-tuning on MED-NODE.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: Not reported. The paper states 'Twenty five epochs were devoted to training the models' and reports results on the MED-NODE dataset but does not specify if a train/test split was used or if the entire dataset was used for both.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No true unseen external test set was used for the primary evaluation. Faces94 is mentioned for ROC visualization but not for quantitative performance reporting.

dataset_level_results.notes: DenseNet201 achieved the highest accuracy. Perfect precision/recall/F1 suggests potential overfitting or data leakage given the small dataset size (170 images) and lack of reported split.
Xception also reported perfect precision, recall, and F1-scores.
EfficientNetB1 also reported perfect precision, recall, and F1-scores.
Lower performance compared to other models.

within_dataset_results.split_or_cv: Not reported (likely full dataset used for evaluation)

generalizability_notes: The study uses a very small dataset (170 images), raising concerns about overfitting and generalizability.
The paper does not report a train/validation/test split, making it unclear if data leakage occurred.
Models like Xception, EfficientNetB1, and DenseNet201 report perfect precision, recall, and F1-scores, which is unusually high for such a small dataset and may indicate data leakage.
No external validation was performed on a held-out clinical cohort.
The comparison with the PECK Ensemble model is indirect, citing results from a separate reference [15].
information_not_reported: Explicit train/validation/test split percentages or counts.
Whether the split was patient-level or image-level (though likely image-level given the dataset nature).
Specific hyperparameter tuning process for model selection.
Performance metrics for the Faces94 dataset despite its mention in ROC analysis.
